literal: self; reflexively, that which one refers to as 'I'; from a root meaning 'breath' (cf. Greek *atmos*) but used in classical Sanskrit purely for the self
invariant_definition: The self in the strict sense: not body, not sense-organ, not *manas*, not *ahaṅkāra*, but the subject for whom experience occurs and to whom ownership is ordinarily ascribed. The term carries a large technical burden. Upaniṣadic inquiry approaches it through negation (*neti neti*), through the three-state analysis of waking, dream, and deep sleep, and through the witness register later called *sākṣin*. Vedāntic debates then ask whether that witnessing self is identical with *brahman*, a mode of *brahman*, or an eternally distinct knower. The term is also sharpened polemically by nearby positions: Sāṅkhya's *puruṣa*, Nyāya's enduring self as substrate of cognition, and the Buddhist denial that any permanent *ātman* can be found behind the five aggregates. In practice, *ātman* is the term one reaches for when the question is not merely who lives this embodied life, but what the referent of 'I' really is once misidentification with body, role, and psychophysical process has been corrected.

school_framing.shared_core: Within the Brāhmaṇical schools, *ātman* names **the strict referent of 'I' after the body-mind complex has been bracketed: the subject for whom experience occurs and who persists through embodiment** (Bṛhadāraṇyaka 2.4, 3.7, 4.3–4). All Brāhmaṇical schools admit such a referent and treat *neti neti* (BṛU 2.3.6) as its delimitative method. They disagree on whether the witness-self is identical with *brahman* (Advaita), a real mode of *brahman* (Viśiṣṭādvaita), an eternally distinct atom-knower (Tattva-vāda, Nimbārka, Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika), or self-luminous consciousness as agent (Trika).
school_framing.register_axes_note: **Same concept, different aspect** among the Vedāntins on the structural role 'strict referent of I'. **Real disagreement** between Advaita and Tattva-vāda/Dvaitādvaita on whether individuation is intrinsic or *upādhi*-conditioned (AF1: same M-register, same scope-inside). Buddhist *anātman* is **a different claim about the same scope**: Mādhyamaka and Yogācāra deny that anything answering to the *ātman*-description is found, so the disagreement is real and stated within the same register (E+Φ).

per_school.definition: *Ātman* is the unborn, non-dual reality, while the plurality of *jīvas* is taught only through *upādhi*-conditioned appearance, as in the *ghaṭākāśa* dṛṣṭānta, and not as any real division in the self [[MK 3.3-7](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.Advaita-prakaraṇa.3-7)]. The self to be known is the *turīya* that is *prapañcopaśama*, *śānta*, and *advaita*, so *ātman* is not a further object within experience but the very non-relational consciousness disclosed by śruti [[MāṇḍU Bh. 7](cite://sankara/mandukya-bhasya/upaniṣad.7)]. Saṃsāra accordingly belongs only to the reflected or superimposed individuality, not to *ātman* itself, which remains untouched by the fate of the *jīva-pratibimba* [[PV p. 65](cite://prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/jīvasya brahma-pratibimbatvāpādanam)].

*Ātman* is *dehātirikta*, eternal, and *ananyasādhana*; it persists as a distinct self in each embodied field and is not reducible to body, senses, *manas*, or cognition [[ĀS 3](cite://yamuna/siddhi-trayam/3)]. Vedānta is said to teach, in one arc, the self's own *svarūpa*, the *antaryāmin-paramātman* whose body the self is, the required *upāsanā*, and the self-manifestation that culminates in limitless *brahmānubhava* [[VS §4](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4)]. The *jīvātman* is of the character of knowledge and bliss, common in kind across selves, while Nārāyaṇa remains the inner ruler of all *cit* and *acit* through a *śarīra-śarīrin* relation articulated by *sāmānādhikaraṇya* [[VS §5](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§5)] [[VS §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)].

*Ātman* is a real *jīva* whose existence, knowledge, liberation, and bliss are all dependent on Viṣṇu's *anugraha*; even where knowledge is intrinsic to the self, *mokṣa* does not arise apart from Hari [[Anuvy. 1.1.13-15](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.13-15)]. Its difference from Īśvara is beginningless and secured both by śruti and by a witness-cognition that apprehends difference, while *abheda*-texts are restricted to *sādṛśya*, *prādhānya*, or honorific transference, never to *svarūpābhidā* [[Anuvy. 2.3.66-69](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69)]. Deep-sleep and departure texts therefore continue to designate *jīva* and Īśvara as distinct, and the *mumukṣu* is directed toward Brahman alone as the knowable [[TD 1.3](cite://raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.3)] [[TD 1.4](cite://raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.4)].
per_school.register_tag: M+S; Tattvavādin [REAL-DISAGREEMENT with Advaita on jīveśvara-bheda]

per_school.definition: *Ātman* is the proper object of *śravaṇa*, *manana*, and *nididhyāsana*, and it is to be *upāsita* as the whole world, so self-knowledge retains an explicitly contemplative and liturgical register [[BṛU 2.4.5](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/2.4.5)] [[BṛU 1.4.15](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/1.4.15)]. The identity-formula *ahaṃ brahmāsmi* excludes a radically external relation between self and Brahman, yet Brahman is also taught under the two forms *mūrta* and *amūrta*; the self is thus situated within a real articulation of unity and differentiation rather than through the cancellation of manifestation [[BṛU 1.4.10a](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/1.4.10a)] [[BṛU 1.4.10b](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/1.4.10b)] [[BṛU 2.3.1](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/2.3.1)] [[BṛU 2.3.6](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/2.3.6)].

Accounts that construe the *jīva* merely through delimitation or reflection are, at best, secondary, since the self's relation to Bhagavān must be stated through his own *svābhāvika acintya-śakti* rather than by a purely external model [[PS §42](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§42)]. The aggregate of selves stands to Bhagavān as ray-atoms to the sun, so that *bheda* and *abheda* are both retained without contradiction [[PS §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. Once Bhagavān is fixed as *māyā-āśraya* and the *jīva* as *māyā-mohita*, the operative *abhidheya* is his *bhajana*, and the self's consummation is *parama-prema* for him as the *parama-svarūpa* of all [[PS §44](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§44)] [[PS §45](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§45)].

*Ātman* is *jñāna-svarūpa*, *aṇu*, dependent on Hari, and distinct in each body, though fit for conjunction with and disjunction from embodiment [[DaŚ 1](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1)]. Its bound, liberated, and devotional conditions are all admitted under beginningless *māyā*, and the self's internal plurality of states and kinds remains doctrinally significant [[DaŚ 2](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/2)]. In systematic exposition, the *jīva* is neither absolutely different nor absolutely non-different from Puruṣottama, but his *aṃśa* as *śakti* and therefore *parādhīna* [[VK 2.3.42](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42)].

*Ātman* is Brahman's own *aṃśa*, known through Vedic revelation and retained as an eternal *jīva* rather than dissolved into an illusory individuality [[BSB 2.3.43-45](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45)]. The emergence of selves and world is not treated as *adhyāsa* upon a featureless absolute, but through *avikṛta-pariṇāma*: Brahman makes itself as itself and yet manifests effect-form without forfeiting its own nature [[BSB 1.4.26](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.4.26)]. The soul reaches Puruṣottama through a *svarūpa-yogyatā* produced by *brahmajñāna* and completed by *bhakti* as the requisite co-operative fitness [[BSB 1.1.12-19](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19)].

*Ātman* is functionally the *puruṣa*: a plurality of conscious subjects inferred from the composite character of psychophysical life, from the inversion between the *guṇas* and awareness, from enjoyership, and from the orientation of practice toward *kaivalya* [[SK 17](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/isk_17)] [[SK 18](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/isk_18)]. The self is accordingly *sākṣin*, *draṣṭṛ*, *madhyastha*, and *akartṛ*; agency belongs to the *guṇas* and their evolutes, while the *puruṣa* remains udāsīna [[SK 19](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/isk_19)]. Liberation is the cessation of misidentification once prakṛti has displayed itself to the *puruṣa* and withdraws, not any real alteration in the self [[SK 59](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/isk_59)].

*Ātman* is rendered here as the *draṣṭṛ/puruṣa*: pure seeing consciousness that, though pure, appears only as the witness of mental presentations [[YS 2.20](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/ys_2.20)]. Its invariance is argued from the fact that the mind's *vṛttis* are always known to their lord; were the *puruṣa* itself *pariṇāmin* like *citta*, those vṛttis would become alternately known and unknown [[YBh 4.18](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/4.18)]. *Kaivalya* is therefore the isolation of *citiśakti* in its own form once the *guṇas* have ceased to serve the *puruṣa*, not the production of a new state in the self [[YBh 4.34](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/4.34)].

*Ātman* is an enduring *dravya* inferred as the substrate of desire, aversion, effort, pleasure, pain, and cognition; these are *liṅgas* that require a continuing subject rather than a mere sequence of bodily events [[NS 1.1.10](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.10)] [[NBh 1.1.10](cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/1.1.10)]. Vaiśeṣika correlatively treats respiration, blinking, vital animation, mental motion, sense-modification, pleasure, pain, desire, aversion, and effort as marks of the self, while the cognition "I" is taken as a direct awareness of the inward self and not merely of the body [[VS 3.2.4](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/3.2.4)] [[VS 3.2.13](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/3.2.13)].

Jaimini takes Vedic acts, absent special restriction, to be for the sake of the *puruṣa*, and Śabara glosses *dharma* as that which joins the person to *niḥśreyasa*; the self is therefore engaged primarily as *adhikārin*, beneficiary, and bearer of ritual consequence [[MS 4.1.3](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/4.1.3)] [[ŚBh 1.1.2](cite://shabara/mimamsa-bhasya/ms_1.1.2)]. At the level of epistemology, perception is the cognition that arises in the *puruṣa* when the senses are in contact with an existent object, so the self is the locus of cognition rather than a merely reducible bodily event [[ŚBh 1.1.4](cite://shabara/mimamsa-bhasya/ms_1.1.4)]. Śabara also argues from desire and recognition across days that the same knower persists, since wishing presupposes prior apprehension by that very subject and the cognition "I saw this" belongs to the inward self rather than to another [[ŚBh, icchayātmānam](cite://shabara/mimamsa-bhasya/icchayātmānam-upalabhāmahe)].

*Ātman* figures here as the locus of *abhisakti* from which the thought 'aham' first arises, and from which the possessive construction 'mama' follows; the text therefore treats self-grasping as the affective root of saṃsāric appropriation [[MAv 1.3](cite://candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.3)]. The philosophical burden falls not on establishing any enduring self-substance, but on undoing the *ātma-mamatā* that governs the wheel-like circulation of the world [[MAv 1.3](cite://candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.3)].

*Ātman* is not a substance standing behind cognition, but an *upacāra* that proceeds within *vijñāna-pariṇāma* itself [[Triṃśikā 1](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/1)]. The reflexive pole that seems to secure selfhood is the *kliṣṭa-manas*, perpetually accompanied by *ātmaduṣṭi*, *ātmamoha*, *ātmamāna*, and *ātmasneha*, so the very appearance of selfhood is already structurally afflicted [[Triṃśikā 6](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/6)].
per_school.register_tag: Φ+E; reflective reader [REAL-DISAGREEMENT: ātman as upacāra in vijñāna-pariṇāma]

per_school.definition: *Ātman* is the already-established Maheśvara who is at once agent and knower in one's own self; the very attempt either to prove or to deny such a subject already presupposes it [[ĪPK 1.1.2](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.1.2)]. Its essential nature is not inert luminosity but *pratyavamarśa*, self-apprehending consciousness, and this reflexive awareness is precisely *svātantrya*, the *aiśvarya* of the *paramātman* [[ĪPK 1.5.13](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.5.13)]. Saṃsārin individuality is only *cidātman* under *śaktisaṃkoca* and *mala*; when that contraction relaxes, the apparently finite subject is known as Śiva himself [[PH 9](cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/9)].

*Ātman* is best rendered here by *jīva*, one of the basic *tattvas*, and its defining mark is *upayoga*, the operative manifestation of consciousness as awareness-and-cognition [[TS 1.4](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/jiva-ajiva-tattvam)] [[TS 2.8](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/upayogo-laksanam)]. Bondage occurs because the passion-bearing *jīva* draws in karmically fit *pudgala*, so the self is neither a bare witness nor a merely verbal person but a real conscious substance enmeshed in material karma [[TS 8.2](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/bandha)]. Liberation is the exhaustive release from karmic matter, correlated with the destruction of deluding and obscuring karmas and the arising of *kevala* [[TS 10.1-2](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/kevala-moksha)].
per_school.register_tag: M+S; reflective reader (jīva as substance with upayoga)

textual_history.summary: The word *ātman* travels from a physical to a metaphysical sense, and the two ends are joined by a genuine semantic continuity rather than by an abrupt jump. The Sanskrit lexeme is connected with a root meaning 'breath' (cf. Greek *atmos* 'vapour'), and the oldest Ṛgvedic uses sit close to that sense: the wind is the *ātman* (breath) that animates, the sun is called 'the ātman of all that moves and stands' (RV 1.115.1), and at death the *ātman* goes to the wind (RV 10.16.3). Already in the Ṛgveda, then, *ātman* names the animating breath-principle and, by extension, the innermost essence of a thing. The Upaniṣads interiorise and deepen this: *ātman* becomes the strict referent of 'I', the self that is sought and that, once found, is identified with brahman — the self 'to be seen, heard, reflected on, contemplated' (Bṛhadāraṇyaka 2.4.5), the self pursued through Prajāpati's long instruction (Chāndogya 8.7ff), the self lodged in the heart and 'smaller than the small' (Kaṭha). The *Bhagavad-Gītā* then deploys *ātman* in two registers at once: the indestructible embodied self, the *dehin* that no weapon cuts (ch. 2), and, in compounds and reflexives, the Self to be governed and known. Unlike *brahman*, whose Ṛgvedic sense (the sacred formula) is semantically distant from its Upaniṣadic sense, *ātman* shows a traceable widening — breath, animating essence, innermost self, the Self that is brahman — so the developmental arc here is closer to genealogy than to mere resonance. Where a link is interpretive rather than textual it is flagged below; note that Aurobindo's *Secret of the Veda*, our Vedic-imagery lens, dwells on the cosmic gods and the Word rather than on *ātman* as such, so it is not pressed into service here.

stages.locus: RV 1.115.1 and RV 10.16.3 (Aufrecht ed.; saved at data/sources/sanskrit/vedic/rigveda_aufrecht_gretil.txt)
stages.sanskrit: citraṁ devānām ud agād anīkaṁ cakṣur mitrasya varuṇasyāgneḥ | āprā dyāvāpṛthivī antarikṣaṁ sūrya ātmā jagatas tasthuṣaś ca || (RV 10.16.3) sūryaṁ cakṣur gacchatu vātam ātmā dyāṁ ca gaccha pṛthivīṁ ca dharmaṇā
stages.rendering: The bright front of the gods has risen, the eye of Mitra, Varuṇa, and Agni; the Sun has filled heaven, earth, and the mid-air — the Sun, the self/breath (ātmā) of all that moves and stands. || (RV 10.16.3, the funeral hymn) Let your eye go to the Sun, your breath/self (ātmā) to the wind; go to heaven and to earth by [the] law, or go to the waters if you are settled there.
stages.note: The earliest pole, and it is physical: *ātman* is the animating breath-principle. In 1.115.1 the Sun is the *ātman* — the life-breath and innermost essence — 'of all that moves and stands'; in the funeral hymn 10.16.3 the dead person's *ātman* is dispersed to the wind (vāta), the breath returning to the cosmic breath. Cf. RV 7.87.2, *ātmā te vāto* ('the wind is your breath/self'). The sense 'breath → animating essence → innermost self' is already latent here; the later 'self' meaning grows out of it by continuous extension, not by rupture.

stages.sanskrit: na vā are sarvasya kāmāya sarvaṃ priyaṃ bhavaty ātmanas tu kāmāya sarvaṃ priyaṃ bhavati | ātmā vā are draṣṭavyaḥ śrotavyo mantavyo nididhyāsitavyo maitreyi | ātmano vā are darśanena śravaṇena matyā vijñānenedaṃ sarvaṃ viditam
stages.rendering: Not for the sake of all, truly, is all dear, but for the sake of the self (ātman) is all dear. The self, truly, is to be seen, to be heard, to be reflected on, to be contemplated, Maitreyī. By the seeing, the hearing, the reflecting, the understanding of the self, all this is known.
stages.note: Here *ātman* is fully interiorised: it is the strict referent of 'I', the one thing for whose sake all else is loved, and the proper object of the contemplative discipline (*śravaṇa-manana-nididhyāsana*) that later Vedānta builds its method on. The breath-sense has receded; what remains is the self as the innermost reality whose knowledge is the knowledge of all. The same chapter's *ahaṃ brahmāsmi* (BṛU 1.4.10) makes this self identical with brahman — the hinge on which the *ātman*=*brahman* equation turns (see the brahman entry's textual_history for the converse).

stages.sanskrit: ya ātmā apahatapāpmā vijaro vimṛtyur viśoko vijighatso 'pipāsaḥ satyakāmaḥ satyasaṃkalpaḥ so 'nveṣṭavyaḥ sa vijijñāsitavyaḥ | sa sarvāṃś ca lokān āpnoti sarvāṃś ca kāmān yas tam ātmānam anuvidya vijānātīti ha prajāpatir uvāca
stages.rendering: The self (ātman) that is free of evil, ageless, deathless, sorrowless, without hunger or thirst, whose desire is the real, whose resolve is the real — that is to be sought out, that is to be inquired into. He attains all worlds and all desires who, having found out that self, knows it — thus spoke Prajāpati.
stages.note: The opening of the long 'search for the self' (Chāndogya 8.7–8.12): Prajāpati's teaching to Indra and Virocana, where the *ātman* is progressively distinguished from the bodily reflection, the dream-self, and the deep-sleep self, until the true self — the unconditioned witness — is reached. This is the canonical Upaniṣadic *analysis* of the self by exclusion (a method the tradition formalises as the three-state inquiry and *neti neti*), the inquiry-side counterpart to the bare identity-statements.

stages.sanskrit: aṇor aṇīyān mahato mahīyān ātmāsya jantor nihito guhāyām | tam akratuḥ paśyati vītaśoko dhātuprasādān mahimānam ātmanaḥ
stages.rendering: Smaller than the small, greater than the great, the self (ātman) of this creature is set down in the secret place (guhā). One free of [self-]will, free of sorrow, beholds it — the greatness of the self — by the grace/clarity of the ordainer [or: of the faculties].
stages.note: The Kaṭha locates the *ātman* in the heart's 'secret place' (guhā) — the same hidden interior where the Ṛgveda lodges the fire and the Taittirīya lodges brahman — and gives it the paradoxical measure 'smaller than the small, greater than the great' (aṇor aṇīyān). The thumb-sized inner self recurs at Kaṭha 2.1.12–13 and 2.3.17. The breath of the Ṛgveda has become the deathless self in the heart; the imagery of the hidden interior is the thread that runs through all three eras.

stages.locus: Bhagavad-Gītā 2.20 and 2.23 (verbatim from the embedded Gītā verses in data/sources/sanskrit/vedanta/shankara_gita_bhasya.txt)
stages.sanskrit: na jāyate mriyate vā kadācin nāyaṃ bhūtvā bhavitā vā na bhūyaḥ | ajo nityaḥ śāśvato 'yaṃ purāṇo na hanyate hanyamāne śarīre || (2.23) nainaṃ chindanti śastrāṇi nainaṃ dahati pāvakaḥ
stages.rendering: It is never born, nor does it die; nor, having been, will it again cease to be. Unborn, eternal, everlasting, ancient, it is not slain when the body is slain. || (2.23) Weapons do not cut it, fire does not burn it.
stages.note: The *Gītā*'s second chapter develops *ātman* as the indestructible embodied self, the *dehin* ('the embodied one', 2.13) that puts on and casts off bodies as a person changes garments (2.22) and that no element can harm (2.23–24). Alongside this metaphysical self the *Gītā* uses *ātman* in an ordinary reflexive and empirical register too — 'raise the self by the self' (6.5), 'the self is the friend of the self' (6.5–6) — so the same word carries both the empirical self to be disciplined and the Self that is unborn. The two-fold use is the *Gītā*'s, not a confusion; the translator must let context fix which is meant (see translator_note).

translator_note: Avoid both 'soul' and an unqualified modern 'self'. 'Soul' imports Christian substance and afterlife assumptions; modern 'self' often suggests personality, autobiography, or the ego-construct that Sanskrit more often names *ahaṅkāra*. *Ātman* names the strict referent of 'I' after the body-mind complex has been bracketed, and many debates turn on whether anything answering to that description exists in the first place.
